My name is Bob Lye and I'm interviewing Clayton Makepeace, who is one of the handful of copywriters in the world, freelance copywriters, who makes more than a million dollars a year and some years two or three million dollars strictly as a freelance copywriter. He copies and he is the, I believe, the highest-paid freelance copywriter in the world and one of only, as I would say, a handful, maybe half a dozen, that earns seven figures. There's a bunch of us below Clayton who are earning six figures anywhere from a hundred to seven hundred, eight hundred thousand a year, but he's in the million dollar category. So this is, to my knowledge, the first time ever we have an opportunity to learn from a million dollar copywriter his secrets for being outrageously successful as a freelance copywriter. So without further delay, I just want to dive into it because we have today an hour. Now the one thing I wanted to get to in terms of housekeeping before we started, not everyone came to this through the total package or through your e-letter. Some came from other sources. So you do publish a now, Clayton, an e-newsletter, correct? That's correct. What's the name of it? We just started it in June and it's called Makepeace Total Package. Actually, it's called the Total Package. If anyone on the call hasn't subscribed, the subscription is free and you're welcome to point your browser to MakepeaceTotalPackage.com. That's M-A-K-E-P-E-A-C-E, TotalPackage, NoSpaces.com. And you'll find an opt-in form there that will get you our weekly issues from now on free. So you've got to get that weekly, but let's get right into it. When I started copyrighting 25 years ago, it was like an unheard of profession. There were a few people doing it, but no one knew what it is. But now it's sort of a hot thing and everybody's saying, oh, this is the greatest job going. Is that true? Is it really good? Well, I think it is. I started back in the early 70s after having worked in television and filmed for a while and found in 74 that there was no work then because of the recession. And so I found a job with an ad agency for $15,000 a year. That doesn't sound like much, but I had no training and I had no experience and I was a high school dropout. After all, $15,000 a year in 74 is the same as $62,000 in $2,500. So it wasn't a bad way to start. And within six years, I was doing over a quarter, well, doing very close to a quarter million dollars a year in today's money. Within eight years, I was over 700,000 in today's money. And in the early 90s, I hit a million dollars a year and never looked back. So did you have a mentor or a coach who got you into that? How did you discover how to do this? No, that's the beauty of it. I had a few books that I read, Reality and the Advertising by Ross Arieves, Scientific Advertising by Claude Hopkins, Tested Advertising Methods by John Cable, Ogilvy on Advertising and Confessions of an Advertising in by David Ogilvy. And then to get the nuts and bolts of the business, I read Successful Direct Marketing Methods by Bob Stone. And that was my foundation in the business. And then after that, I just really started watching what people were mailing and studied each package very carefully. Today, everybody on this call, I think, is far more fortunate than I was because I had to learn and make it up as I went along. There weren't coaches, there were no easing, telling you how to do it. There was nothing. And so I think copywriters starting today really have a leg up. Also, a lot of top copywriters today are taking on copycups and doing mentoring with them. As a way to extend their own income, they're accepting beginning writers as copycups and training them. I've done that six or seven times now. And each one of those writers is a mid to high six-figure writer or a million-dollar writer. Can you name a couple of them or is it confidential? Not at all. No, Carleen Anglick Cole, I think my readers are familiar with it, the name, came to me in the mid 1990s. She was making six figures a year as a marketing assistant with photos publishing, but she was not a trained copywriter by any stretch of the imagination. And she asked me if, as a friend, I would help her break into this business. And I did in her very first year. She made more as a copywriter than she had been making with her six-figure salary at Phillips. Kent Comey came to me. He was a five-figure writer just getting started. And I hired him to help me with the work I had at Weiss Research. And Kent is now a solid six-figure writer, the same is true with Bob Hutchinson. Paris Lampropoulos took off like a house of fire and is making seven figures now as a copywriter and has his own copycups. And there are a couple of others, Brian London, who now is a refugee from New Orleans living in Baton Rouge after the hurricane, made enough money as a copywriter that he bought Jefferson Financial from his boss and now runs a New Orleans investment conference and has several newsletters and trading services that he publishes. I hired Brian from a radio station where he was writing spots for local customers, clients. So of the six or seven people I've worked with over the last five or six years, every one of them has done very, very well for themselves. And so, you know, I've just, I've seen it, it's happened to me, I've seen it happen to other people. And so, you're not an anomaly, as they say in the matrix. It can be done. Not only can it be done, but these young guys are doing it faster than I did. You know, it took me a considerable amount of time to hit the million-dollar mark. Paris did it in a matter of just a few years. Now, speaking of people who start out, who are younger or are starting out, maybe after another career. The guys you all mentioned work for major clients, but for many people listening to this call, especially novices, the idea of going out and contacting and selling yourself to a major client is very intimidating. For example, when you started, how did you overcome this? It was a tough one for me because right now promoting the total package means I have to promote myself and my background, my experience. That's the hardest thing I've done in probably 20 years because it's just not my nature to do that. The same was true when I was getting started with copywriting. I had tremendous insecurities and stage fright. When I would be faced with, fortunately, I was unemployed. When I went freelance, by definition, I was unemployed. I had a wife and two kids and an overhead I had to meet every month. I would wake up on the first of each month with no money coming in. I had no choice but to bang the phone and to contact clients and say, here I am. I had a lot of problem with that. It was not easy because I'd be talking to a marketing manager at KCI, which was about Kefhart's company back in the old days. I would have seen their promotion pieces for months and was impressed by them and was studying them and trying to learn from them. Here I was on the phone with the copy chief who directed the production of that piece. It was pretty daunting. I found out very early on that they were more interested, or at least as interested, in most cases, more interested in talking to me than I was to them because their jobs were dependent on finding great copywriters to promote their products. But at the time, you may have been great in terms of your raw talent, but you were not a proven force at that time, were you? Absolutely not. So why would they talk to you? It all turned around for me when I realized that sales copy is the one missing ingredient that these companies need to promote their existing products and to add new products. They can't grow without great sales copy. After I learned that, I learned that these people have committees and meetings and managers whose only job is to go out there, beat the bushes, and find the next hot copywriter. If you can come in with some good ideas, a couple of good samples of your work, and then talk to them about how you would improve what they're doing now, give them a few ideas, it costs them more not to test you than it does to go ahead and give you a try. So you're saying, which I think is a revelation to a lot of people in this call, that rather than when the copywriters in this call contact these publishers rather than test an annoyance, we're actually helping them do their job. Their job is to look for this. Yes. When a marketing manager at a major direct marketing company, direct response company of publisher of vitamin sales company, whatever, finds a copywriter who proves to be an asset to that company, it's a feather in that marketing manager's hat. The person who first hired me to do a health promotion at Phillips, the person who first hired Gary Ben-Savenga to do a financial promotion at Phillips, these people now are heroes. Finding great writers, got them the corner offices, the company cars, the big bonuses, and all the things that go along with moving up in a company. Well I understand they're looking, but when you're a beginner and you call them, beyond the fact that they're looking, then the question becomes, well why should they be interested in you? How did you overcome that as a beginner? Did you have some great samples? Did you create some great samples? Once obviously they had a need, but how did you sell you Clayton as the solution to that need? I understand how you do it now. You have a great track record, but back then I don't think you did. You were a beginner, right? No, I did. The first thing that I did really, in fact, everyone who's done this call I hope has received the two bonus reports that we offered with the call. One of the bonuses is how to get paid to build a power pack portfolio. In there I talk about exactly how I did it, thought. There were several ways. One was the challenge. I picked up a phone one day. I remember I had $70 in the bank because I had just returned a rental truck that I had used to move my family from Los Angeles to Crescott, Arizona. I picked up the phone and I called a publisher I knew of in Phoenix who I was just dying to write for. He had seven or eight newsletters that he promoted. It was Johnny Johnson, a research publication. I just told him on the phone that I thought his package stuck and that I could beat it. By the time I got off the call I had an assignment. I had offered him to write the package for him for free and that he would only pay me if it worked. I felt at the time I had to make an offer like that for that very reason. He didn't know me. I didn't have a big portfolio. I had some direct marketing promotions I had written including an audition I had done earlier for an advertising agency and a few product promos for everything, water heaters, restaurants, Beverly Hills, those kinds of things that I had picked up. I had no newsletter promotion. I basically used this offer that was too good to refuse. Let me spend a month of my life trying to create a breakthrough for you. If it doesn't work, you don't know the extent. Was risk-free? It was a no-brainer. He went for it. He absolutely went for it. Within a year I was doing promotions for all seven of his newsletters. The question would be though, that was then, this is now. A lot of copywriters email me and they say, I'm very worried. I look on the internet and it seems there's all these AWAI students and there's all these copyrighting boot camps and there's so many copywriters today. Now is it different? Is there too many copywriters or is the demand still greater than the supply and copyrighting? You would know, so tell us. Sure. I had a conversation with Ed Elliott at Phillips Publishing today. He's a creative director. He's in charge of finding new talent. In fact, he's been calling me, pursuing me at least weekly for the last month to help him find new writers. This is Phillips. They pay substantial royalties. They pay solid advances. They're willing to try young writers and they're desperate for new talent. They have a very large product line. They're wanting to add new products. They can't do it until they get new writers. They're desperate. So, Phillips is one of the Rolls Royces, is the whole publishing industry. So I know that it's still the case. Now, if you go in there and you have nothing that has mailed for another client, but you do, especially for another newsletter or supplement company, but you have an audition piece you wrote. Explain what you mean by an audition piece. An audition piece would be something that I think our AWAI students are very, very familiar with. We call them spec assignments. Right. Well, there are spec assignments and there are spec assignments. The projects that you actually do is a student of AWAI and there are some other writing courses as well. Right. And as part of your education, you've created these assignments. Then there are the spec assignments where you actually look at a product in a promotion piece that, let's say, a Phillips is doing. You pick a newsletter, let's say it's Health and Healing, Julian Whitaker's newsletter, and you look at the package they're mailing to promote that and you take as long as you want because they don't know you're doing it and you write the best promotion that newsletter has ever had, the best promotion you know how to write. And then once it's done, you send it to Ed Elliott over at Phillips or you do it for a Gory. You do it with Jenny Thompson at the Health Group at Agora or one of the other creative directors or group publishers at these major publishing companies. You do that, you tell them that you'd like to talk to them about it and you get them on the phone and you say, look, you know, I just wrote this package for you. I think it's got great potential. I think it can be what you're mailing now. And one of several things will happen. They'll either say, absolutely, won't you want this package, how much you want for it? That's when you get to tell them what your deal is, when you get to that in a minute. Or they'll say, you know, it's close but no cigar, but you know, we'd like to work with you a little bit on it. We think it has potential. And now you've got a copy chief at a major publisher who's going to work with you through the copy. It's going to be, they're basically going to pay you to learn. Now there are two educations. The education that you pay for and there's the education you get in the real world where people pay you. And that's what starts when this kind of thing happens. The worst thing that they can do is just basically say, ah, you know what, and they'll be nice, believe me. They'll say, well, we just don't have need for a writer right now. They're lying through their teeth, but they're trying to be nice to you. What they're really saying is you're not quite there yet. So what do you do in that case? You say, that's great. Well, you know, and ask them for suggestions. What do you think I could do to become the kind of writer that you would want to hire? And these people are nice people. You know, after 30 years with these folks, they're friends of mine. They went to my wedding. They, you know, we, when we get on the phone, we chat for way much longer than we should about family and about other things. These are nice people and they're highly motivated to discover the next great writer. So my suggestion is take a deep breath and take the bullets and say, you know what, I really appreciate that. You know, you're being quite honest with me here. I was just wondering what I, what you might think I might do, who I might talk to, what were the weak spots you saw in the copy? You know, pick their brain and they'll be more than happy to talk to you about it. Now, if they don't jump on what you've said and say, yeah, we want to mail this, how much is there, how can you convince a relic and client who's maybe sitting on the fence and says, well, maybe you could work out to give you that shot? Well, that's kind of the art of the deal again. You can, you can simply offer to finish the package to their satisfaction for no upfront fee and for a royalty. You can offer to do this one for them at a substantially reduced royalty, whereas normally you would ask $30 a thousand for a health package or $40 a thousand. I think $30 is about reasonable. The, on this one, you'll say, look, I tell you what, not only when I charge you a fence, you help me finish this. You copy chief need to finish this and I'll only take 10 bucks a thousand. I know it'll take a little longer, it'll take a little more of your time, but I think it's a good investment. I think you'd be surprised how many people will accept that kind of an offer. Let's look at those fee arrangements. First, there is the royalty only and you're saying royalty can range from anywhere from 10 to 30 bucks per thousand. Sure. Hey, Bob, before I jump into that, let me just add one last thing. When you do one of those spec assignments, say you do the one for health and healing when we were just discussing, when you send it in to Ed Elliott over at Phillips, go ahead and send it to Jenny Thompson and other people too and just tell them, here's a package I'm creating for a Phillips at Health and Healing. It's a rough draft still, but it's in process and it's a sample of my work. The thing that you wrote for Phillips could generate money for you, but it's from Phillips, but it could also be a sample or a sales tool that you can show around the industry and let people know that you're a serious writer and that you're talking to people who are hiring serious writers. If you show it to people, let's say you do it for a Phillips publication and you show it to people other than Phillips like Agora or SoundView, do you tell them it's a spec assignment or do you not say anything about it? I would not. If they ask about what kind of arrangement you have with Phillips, I would absolutely go ahead and be honest. It's a very small and incestuous business. All of the competitors know each other. They go to the same conferences and they talk to each other and they compare notes on copywriters. They'll never tell you if they have a hot new copywriter. They will not tell the competitor that, but what they will tell them is if they're working with a copywriter and it's well known and they'll say, well, you know, he's spotty or not consistent or he's really good, if that's already well known. But just be aware that your clients talk to each other. They hang out with each other. They go to the same conferences and anything you say to a client, there's a very, very good chance it's going to be repeated to other prospective clients. The other thing is you have to be a little careful when you're doing this kind of thing because you don't want to post it on the internet or blast it out in big mailings to people because if Phillips does accept your work and give you an assignment, then they're not going to appreciate the fact that you've taken copy. They're about to mail and show it to all of their competitors. But so long as they don't have a commitment to you, so long as they haven't told you that they are absolutely going to mail it, you can get by with it. Just be a little bit subtle about it. That makes sense. Now you are, I think, because I've heard you, a really good negotiator and you seem to negotiate and arrange your own deals. And you've said to me that there are about half a dozen different combinations and permutations of fee arrangements for copywriters under which the top people work. Can you run through those for us? Sure. When I first got started, basically, we were working on flat fees. Believe it or not, in the 70s, to write a direct mail package, I got $600 flat. And that was right in 8 to 16 page sales letter. Everything was enveloped back then. Nobody used that. And our industry, at least, was using self-mailers, mag logs, and tabloids. And so I got $600 to do it. And tab, insult to injury, there was no Federal Express. So if I had to get something to a client quickly, I had to buy a seat on an airplane for it. The only fax machines we had were these little quips machines. And it took three minutes to transmit one page. And you had to load each page separately and they sunk to high heavens when they were doing it. And we were writing on IBM's Selectric Typewriters. So every draft meant a complete retighting of the entire project. And for that, we got $600 flat fee. For new writers, especially working with the internet and especially working with local clients and companies that are not familiar with royalty arrangements, a flat fee is a good option because it's a great way to get some samples in your portfolio. So flat fee? Yes. Another way to go is royalty only, which we've just discussed. It's a great way for a young copywriter to get going. You simply go in and you say, look, I'm not even going to charge you in advance because you have no way of knowing how great my copy is going to be and the fact that it's going to earn out like crazy. And earn out is when a publisher pays you in advance on your royalties. He calculates how many pieces he's going to have to mail of that in order to reimburse him for the advancing page. So a lot of packages don't earn out. If you charge a $25,000 advance and a $25,000 royalty, they're going to have to mail, what is it, a million pieces in order to get their money back. So a lot of packages don't earn out. And that's particularly a problem with young, unproven writers. So offering to write for royalty only is an excellent way to build your portfolio and to get going. Okay, so flat fee then royalty only, and then you mentioned the idea of an advance plus royalty. Is that what most people do? Most of the people I know work on an advance plus royalty. And essentially the advance and the royalty are established in negotiation between the copywriter and the client. And from a copywriter standpoint, you're interested in how large this guy's mailing universe is and what the mailing potential is. If you hit an out of the park home run for the guy, if you do as well as his controller or slightly better, then how many pieces is he capable of mailing, given his mailing universe? The mailing universe, of course, is the total number of qualified prospects for the product that you're promoting that appear on mailing lists or email lists. And so if the mailing universe is gigantic, I generally give my clients a bit of a break on the royalty amount. And so for example, the health market where there's 280 million Americans who have physical bodies and some large percentage of them are fairly interested in keeping those bodies healthy and have qualified themselves by purchasing products from other health companies. And therefore their names are available to my clients to rent and mail my package to. That's a huge market. And so I'm willing to take a $30,000 royalty when I write a package for the health market. On the other hand, the investment market, the investment information market is substantially smaller. And so I require a $50,000 royalty to write a package for an investment information product, a newsletter or a book, something on general investment. If it's on personal finance, again, everyone has money and there are huge lists of Rodell and Bordroom of people who have qualified themselves by buying books and newsletters on how to save money, how to cut your monthly budget, how to save more, how to get out of debt, how to cut your taxes, those kinds of things. Then again, the smaller royalty is in order because of the size of the universe. So is the money you get paid upfront an advance against royalty or just a flat fee that's in addition to the royalty? I use several different arrangements. Quite often I use the money upfront as an advance. I offered for the money upfront to be an advance against the royalty and I received half when they reserved my time up to two years in advance. They only have an opening in 2007, but if they want that opening, they're going to have the payment $12,500 today, which is half of the $25,000 advance. Then the balance when I either begin work in some cases, that's with clients who know me well. When I deliver a first draft in other cases, for people who know me pretty well but still have some reservations as to whether I'll actually deliver copy or not, or on approved final copy for the clients I haven't worked for before in that assurance that not only will I deliver the copy, but it'll be powerful when I do. Another arrangement is the fee plus royalty. That's when you're getting cocky. That's when you've been around for a while and you say, look, there's no point in an advance. You know this is going to earn out. I've got a fantastic track record. Gary Benson-Vengan writing a financial package, which thank God he's not doing anymore. He was a stiff competitor. Or Jim Rott in the financial or health areas. Or me in the supplement areas. You ask for a fee plus royalty. The fee is free and clear of any future deductions for royalties. You get to keep the fee and then beginning with the very first piece mailed you begin receiving your royalty. You've also mentioned to me that sometimes you have a commission that's based or a fee that's based on a percentage of not the, it's not based on the number mailed, but it's based on the actual sales of the product. How does that work? Well, on a lot of the projects that you're going to be doing as a copywriter, well they fall into two categories. The first category are direct mail and packages, internet promotions and so forth that are designed to generate new customers. So the copy that you're writing is going to be sent out to prospects that are not currently customers of your client. And those nailings and internet promotions are quite often done on a break-even basis. In other words, the client is willing to continue mailing a copy so long as he gets his marketing cost back from the promotion. And sometimes you're willing to lose a little bit of money. Yeah, I can name several. Yeah. So what does that mean? Well, it means that they're going to be nailing that quantity. Right? And it means that they're going to be, if they're shooting for break-even, it means essentially that 100% of the revenues that promotion generates are going to be plowed back into the mail. Right. Okay? So you're talking huge quantities. And on deals like that, you want to tie your royalty to the number of pieces mailed. And in the old days, we always talked in per 1,000. Today, a lot of people talk about per cent, a sense per piece mailed. Right. They'll say, you said $10 to $30 per 1,000, which is a penny to three pennies a piece. Exactly. And for the smaller markets, like the financial markets, a nickel a piece. A few years ago, though, of Weiss research, a great client of mine and Martin's a great friend, hired me to do some promotions to his customer file. Well, he only had 120,000 customers. And it would take me just as long to write a promotion for his customers as it would to write a promotion to generate new customers that I knew he was mailing 18 million pieces a year of my packages and paying huge royals. Right. So that promotion going into the customers, if he sends it to every customer, it's only mailing 120,000 pieces. That's right. So even if you're making two or five cents a piece, it's not significant to you. Exactly. And so what I did was, I said to him, hey, Martin, look at this. You know, you're paying $500 per thousand to mail your direct mail pieces to new customers. My royalty is $50 a thousand, roughly 10% of the money that promotion will generate because he was mailing at break even. And so he said, I will be more than happy to write for your house file for 10% of the revenue that my promotion generates. It's gross revenue. The gross revenue that my promotion generates is correct. Now in his case, gross revenue and net revenue were very similar because he was selling electronically delivered products. He had no printing costs or mailing costs in terms of fulfillment and no marketing costs because everything we were writing, we were emailed last. Right. And so I said, you're paying me 10% of revenues in the mail. I think you should pay me 10% of revenues when I promote on the internet. And he said yes. And the rest is history. What about, you mentioned to me in conversation something called an override. How does that work? Yeah, this is not always easy to do and some clients will resist it. But once you have some winners with a client, I did this with Weiss and it worked out wonderfully. I went to my client and I said, look, I'm generating 10, 15, sometimes 20,000 new customers for you every month and I'm doing it for $50,000 royalty. You're breaking even on those names. Those new customers are costing you nothing. Then right away you're turning around and you're creaming those names. You're mailing strong electronic promotions to them offering $5,000 trading services, $10,000 trading services and other very lucrative products. And I said, I think I deserve a portion of the revenue that my customers that I generated for you are bringing you. And I had the added benefit that Dan Rosenthal and I were kind of tag-teaming the client and Dan was doing some new customer acquisition stuff. I was doing some, Dan was doing some back-end stuff and now Martin wanted me to do some. And so I said, look, why don't we do this? Let me take over the front-end entirely. I'll get you all your new customers. But then I'm not going to stand around while you and Dan make all the money on the back-end. I want an over-rise of 5% on every purchase the customers I generate from you make. And then I'll be out of your hair on the back-end until you need me. If you want me to come in, but I won't be hounding you to give me back-end assignments because that's where the money is. And he said, okay. And so for doing absolutely nothing extra other than writing the promotions for the front-end I was doing anyway. I collected a nice 5% override on all back-end sales. So I get these options. Does the copywriter just, especially if you're a new copywriter, do you just pick one of these options and tell the client this is what I charge? Or do you give the client several choices? Well, if I were beginning today I would probably begin with an advanced plus royalty arrangement. And I would probably start out offering or asking for something in the range of $15,000 advance, $12,000 to $15,000 advance against a royalty of $12,000 to $15,000 a thousand. If you want to, and this is important to understand, if your copy doesn't kick butt, your royalty costs the client nothing, zero. There's some clients out there that will say, well, we don't pay new writers that. Well, that is just feacious. The fact of the matter is that as a new writer you can go in and you can say, you know, I want a $50,000 royalty. And if the client says, well, we don't pay new writers that, you just say, well, you know, you won't have to pay it if I don't beat what your experienced writers are doing. You know, if I do, I'm worth it. If I don't, it costs you nothing. What do you have to lose? Right? Interesting point, way to present that. And then a lot of people will say to you, and this was actually said to me by a good friend who's a marketing director the other day, but, you know, new writers aren't consistent. And so we pay the higher royalties to experienced writers and writers that we have a track record with because we know we have a higher percent chance of getting a control when we work with that person. Again, a totally specious argument because consistency has nothing to do with it. The royalty is not paid unless the package wins. If your package generates a profit or generates a customer at a break even, your copy is worth exactly the same to that client as a copy that works not that well or that well written by an A-level writer. So negotiating down advances or flat fees because of lack of consistency or history or track record or experience of a young writer, that is an arguable point. It's logical. But when someone tells you that the customer you generate doesn't have the same value to that company as the customer that Gary Benavigo or I or Jim Rods or Arthur Johnson generate, I'm sorry, he's just haggling at that point. He's horse trading and he doesn't believe it either. Got it. By the way, we just had a question emailed us. You had used the term, you had suggested that you would approach a large publisher and tell them, hey, you helped me finish this promotion. You copy chief me and a student emailed us and says, what do you mean by copy chief? Okay. A copy chief is simply somebody, either a mentor, an A-level writer or in some cases a marketing director at a client company who will, acknowledging that you are a junior writer or just beginning or even an experienced writer but new to this field, who will agree to work with you to develop the theme, the headline, the organization of the package and who will work with you to make the package successful. He'll critique each draft. We call it critting. He'll crit each draft. Dan Rosenthal is the most famous critter in the universe. The guy is brutal. He takes a sadistic joy and absolutely eviscerating copy. In fact, his email address is copy ogre. Anyway, the copy chief will take you and take you through a series of projects. If he's not a client who's doing this, if he's an A-level writer or a coach, he'll typically take a portion of the advance and a portion of the royalty. Depending on your experience level, that portion can be substantial or less. If you're pretty close and he knows that he has a good chance of earning royalties when he works with you, then he'll be likely to ask for a lower percentage of the royalty in advance. On the other hand, he sees 27 drafts ahead of him. Even then, a so-so chance of getting a royalty out of it, then he'll want more. What's the best way for someone who is fairly much of a beginner and doesn't have a big track record and hasn't written a lot of promotions? Let's say you wanted to go over the newsletter field and you haven't worked for a newsletter publisher before. Is your best bet, if I could do one thing, is it to go find an A-level writer and apprentice to him or is it to do, as you said, pick a product from a major publisher, write a package and show it to them? Like show Ed Elliott a promotion for one of his supplements? I really think it's kind of all of you both. It's like DC fishing. You've got to have a lot of lines in the water. In the total package, actually, I think it was the landing page for this teleconference. I talked about the six-step program I used to recruit new clients back in the very late 70s, very early 80s when I first went out and began freelancing. That worked like a charm in that it not only got me assignments, it got my name out there and I was able to get samples out. I had that going on. At the same time, I was calling publishers I wanted to work for and then salting their packages and challenging them to let me beat them, as we discussed earlier with Johnny Johnson, a research publication. At the same time, I was taking assignments from clients I really didn't want. That were a pain in the neck, but it gave me a sample to show. How important is it at the beginning to get samples? Is that more important than the money you make for the first six months or a year? It depends on where you are in the process, really. But samples are at least as expensive, as valuable as the money in the early going. Obviously, you can't eat samples and like me, you're trying to support a wife and a couple of kids in a world's dumbest cocker spaniel while trying to get your business going. You obviously need the income as well. But samples are money in the bank. They're absolute money in the bank. The more that you can put in your portfolio. Again, at first, I had samples where I did a promotion for a point of use water heater. The kind of water heater they put in hot tubs. I had an airplane. I had one for a shop that sold exotic desserts in Beverly Hills. All kinds of dumb stuff. I had some fundraising samples that I had done to raise funds for Proposition 13 in California. I showed those to my newsletter clients. Did they respond to that? Yes. Look, anybody who does direct marketing can look at copy and know whether it's hot copy or not. They'll read it and they'll know intuitively, this makes me want to buy, this makes me excited about the product. I can see this person writing for my newsletter and doing a bang up job. I think that's a major point and I want to just emphasize it. You're saying that you can impress, let's just take newsletters as an example. You can impress a major publisher of newsletters with really good copy even if that copy is not selling newsletters. Absolutely. Absolutely. Young copywriters send their samples all the time. I can literally spend 30 seconds scanning a sample and knowing and know right where this person is and exactly how much work it would take on my part to get a finished package out of him that will be a winner, him or her. I did this with two ladies very recently. I don't care if it's about tapes or records or lawn mowers or vitamin supplements. It doesn't matter. If the person has a handle on the dominant resident emotion of their market, if they're speaking to the benefits in an exciting, compelling way and they're making not ordering feel like the dumbest thing I could do, then this is somebody that I want to be in business with. Absolutely. I would take every assignment I can get. I would make up assignments if I couldn't get some. But it's a stepping stone process at first to some degree. Every once in a while you're going to hit like with the six-step program that I've outlined in the total package landing page. Every once in a while somebody will say, you know what? I do think you could do this. Here's what we pay. Is that okay with you? Let's do one together and see what happens. The more lines you have in the water, the more likely that is to happen for you. Sooner or later we'll cover everything in the total package, but I think the people who were kind enough to pay to attend this call should be the ones that get the benefit of those answers. Well, let's get to one of those questions that just came in because it was about what we were discussing. Are royalties based on the number of pieces mailed or on the response to the mailing? Okay, the response to the mailing determines the number of pieces mailed. Right, because if it doesn't do well they're not going to keep mailing it. That's right. A client, as I mentioned earlier, a client has basically a red line. His boss or his accounting department has told him that you're, and by him I mean him or her, the marketing manager. Given that marketing manager an objective, and the objective might be, for example, I want new customers for free, which means when I do my promotion I want all the money I spend on that promotion back and I want a new customer. That means that that marketing manager can mail just in a mail, every mailing list out there that will come in at break even or factors mailing. He mails some losers too, but the overall effect of the mailing is return on investment is 100%. He gets 100% of his money back. Then others will get more aggressive and say, we'll lose a little bit of money because we have this great back end program we're going to be selling, so we don't mind coming in at 80% or 90% because we know in the first 60 days we'll recover that money and then that client's with us seven years and he's going to spend money like crazy. They have a different criteria. Still others, and these are the ones I don't understand. They say we want to make money on every new client. Those are the ones you've got to be careful about because if the guy tells you that he wants to make a profit on every client, he wants a return on investment for the mailing of 120% or 140%, then that means he's going to mail a lot fewer pieces. The relationship between response and promotion size is a direct relationship and the higher the response or the, let's say for example a guy's mission in life is to break even on new customers and he mails your package and it pulls a 300% or 400% return on investment on test lists. Most clients will test the better files, better outside files on a new package to lower their risk of losing money when they test in packages. You pull 400% on his best outside file. Then he goes back to his office and sits down with his mailing list history and he looks at how every other list he's ever mailed indexes against the list that he just tested your package to and he immediately knows how many million pieces of that package he could mail. Right? Right. So, your response percentage determines mail size and so you're being rewarded for the effect. Right, but the royalty itself is actually based on the mail size, the number of pieces mailed as you would put it, it's $10, $20, $30, $50 per thousand pieces mailed. That's right. Or a penny to a nickel per piece. It's Tinker's Devers DeChance. The response rate you produce determines the size of the mailing and your royalty is connected to the size of the mailing, either per piece mailed or on a per thousand basis. And so, that's how it works. Now in our original promotion for this conference, we had a bullet point that I don't think we've answered yet. The bullet, and you wrote it actually, was how being a direct response bully can make you rich. So, who's the bully? I suspected you. Yeah, well actually that was a kind of flowery way of discussing something we have talked about. And that's looking...one of the best things you can do as a copywriter. And it's not easy for people who are starting out because money's tight, but get on the mailing list of the competitors, of the companies that you want to work for. If you want to work, for example, in the financial publishing industry, cough up the box and take a one-year subscription to one of the newsletters offered by each of the major players. And it doesn't cost a lot because each of them has a front-end newsletter that's only $49 a year or so. Right. Some of them are as high as $90. Yeah, but that's it. Yeah, that's it. And you get on those lists. And then what happens is every time that list is rented to a competitor, every time a gore, say you subscribe to...Oxford Club. Yeah, or Oxford Club or Strategic Investing over at Agora, well, next month, within 30 days, you're going to get a promotion from Phillips because Agora rents her list to Phillips. And you're going to start seeing all of the promotion pieces that Phillips does for their product. You're going to see the promotion pieces that everybody at Agora rents that list to do for their product. Right. And then you're filling up. And you're going to be disappointed on days you go to the mailbox and there's nothing there. I mean, it almost never happens. You'll be getting a lot of mail. And so that gives you an opportunity to see everyone's promotion pieces out there. And not just their controls, you'll be seeing test panels as well. Right. Keep that in mind when you're looking at these things because you may look at something and then this thing really stinks. I can beat the living daylights out of it. When you call them up and tell them that, they may say, yeah, I know, that was a test at Bomb. See now? So be prepared for that. But look through those packages and then be a bully. Pick the wimpy as kid on the block, the one that you're sure you can whip in a New York minute and approach the client with that and say, well, were you thinking? You know, and explain to him why you can beat it. And I tell you, it's amazing. One of my major clients sent me two promotion pieces that they had mailed over within the last year and had worked and asked me to critique them and tell them why I thought they didn't work. And I spent maybe a half hour just going through these pieces looking at them. And they were copy chief by strong, creative people internally. They miss the market almost every level. And I'm not going to say which client that was, but it's not my socks off because I have very, very high respect for the people who copy chief and supervise the production of this package. So just because the company is huge and just because they're mailing something doesn't mean it's great and doesn't mean it's working well. So go ahead and be a bully. Take on the weakest packages out there and come up with a plan on how you would feed it. And then go to the group publisher or the marketing director at that company and strike up a conversation about it. That's worked very well for me many times in the past. The great advice. Another question that just came out that says, how do we go about finding the client companies that use and need copywriters? And I think you've kind of answered that just now, didn't you? Well, there are several ways actually. I tell you what, I found, first of all, that niche writers make the most money. It's like a cardiologist makes more money. Specialists. Yes. Right. A cardiologist makes more money than a general practitioner. Right. And in copywriting, it's really the same thing. If you have a specialty in financial writing, a lot goes with that. I mean, you don't have to just be a good copywriter. You need to know the difference between a mutual fund and an exchange-traded fund. You need to know what price earnings ratios and price-to-book ratios are. You need to know why the market goes up, why it goes down. You need to know about economic trends. There's a lot of specialized knowledge that goes into that. And the same is true in the health area. You don't have to necessarily know about which alternative remedies work the best, because the editor will tell you that. But when he starts talking about an embolism, you need to know what that is. Right. And when he starts talking about a cardiac infarction, you need to know any heart attack. So there's a lot that goes into becoming a specialist in each of these fields. But to answer this question specifically of where do you find the clients, let me give several suggestions, see if you agree. First of all, there are directories that cover the direct marketing industry. And there's one called the Directory of Major Mailers, which is a massive book that lists almost every company that does direct marketing in any volume. I believe it's available if you search directory of major mailers on the internet. You can get it online and you can purchase it on CD or in paper. And that'll give you thousands of companies. You can find out exactly what they sell and what they mail and how many packages. Absolutely. I'm looking at it now. It's a target marketing group. And you can go to majormailers.com, www.majormailers.com. Books about three inches thick. And not only does it tell you who the major mailers are, but it tells you what they're mailing. And notice, folks, Clayton doesn't need clients. Just the opposite. And he owns the directory of major mailers. Well, actually, I want to offer this to total package subscribers, which is why I bought it. OK. Yeah. Well, that's cool. And then if you're interested, for example, in newsletter publishers, you can search www.newsletters.org. And that's the website of NEPA, which is the major trade association for business and consumer newsletter publishers. So those are two sources you can use. Right. Also the direct marketing association. The DMA. Right. You have, I'm not that familiar with their stuff. I haven't looked at it in a long time, because I haven't been dealing with bushes for a long time. But they do conferences, and they quite often have directories of exhibitors. And I'm sure they also have a directory of members. Right. And if you have a local chapter of the direct marketing association, you join that local chapter. It's a great way to network. And usually when you join a local chapter, they give you the membership directory, which will typically have 100 or 200 potential clients with all their information listed. Right. But getting back to what I was saying, as you go through this, it would be one thing to see this directory of major mailers, for example, go to every, list just about everybody out there. I would just suggest that you pick a niche, educate yourself in it, focus on those niches. Definitely. Look at what's mailed to you. And what turns you on? Like Clayton is very into financial and alternate supplements. And some people are interested in commodities trading and options trading. The more you... I've always found this to be true, too. Specialists have an easier time getting work and are paid more than generalists. Absolutely. Arthur Johnson got started really at Franklin Mint selling collectibles. And that was his first experience. And he really made a name for himself in that field. That is a big niche, collectibles. Eric Bittell, who's one of the all-time great writers, he's in the Pantheon, he's in the Hall of Fame, four or five best writers of all time. He writes soft offer, his specialty, he writes everything, but his specialty, what he almost never misses at, is a soft offer, one-shot book promotion. And he's produced many of the biggest winners that Rodale and Bordroom have ever put out. So you see, you can really specialize even deeper, not just books, but soft offer, one-shot book promotions. Right. We're just about out of time. We have two minutes. So I want to ask one last question, and then we can head toward wrapping up. In the promotion we sent out, you also mentioned the number one blunder new copywriters make that turns clients off like crazy every time. What is that? Being too available. I saw a promotion, it was an ad that ran in, I think it was DM News. We talked about this, we should not mention the person's name. No, we won't. And the ad basically was a copywriter promoting himself. When you promote yourself like that broadly, you're sending a message that basically you're available. Clients are kind of like Rodney Dangerville once said that, why would I join any club that would accept me as a member? Right. Well, clients have the same question in their minds. They're saying why would I hire any copywriter who has immediate time in his schedule? And so you have to have a reason why you're soliciting business now. And one of the things that I always did when I was trying to fill my schedule, and I wanted to get some advances in to pay the bills and to get some bookings, is I would contact clients and say, look, I fill my schedule just twice a year. And I'm booked for the next six months, or three months, or whatever the time period is, but I'm booking my schedule up for the first six months of next year. I'm sending this letter to seven or nine or 12 or whatever it was, other clients that I work with. And the slots are going first come, first serve. So in other words, you have to have a rationale. There has to be a reason. There has to be a reason why you don't want to be the ugliest girl at the dance. You want to be the one that everybody has to hustle to get on your dance. The late Howard Shenson called this the busy doctor syndrome. He said, clients always want to deal with those who feel busy and successful, not those who seem to be desperate and need the work. We're out of time, but my one distress is that I think we could have gone on another two or three hours. We do more of these with you. What's our going to be our opportunity? I feel like we didn't finish. Like we were just scratching the surface. Yeah, I think so too. And I've got like 21 more things I wanted to cover on this call. Plus, we have all of the questions. Bob, if you'd be willing to do it, I'd like to start doing this on a regular basis. OK, why don't we talk about that and then we whatever we decide, we can send out an email to the people here and to the people who get the total package. And let's do that. Let's do a couple. Let's do a series of calls so we can get into a lot of this in much more detail. I would like I'm thinking three or four calls would be good. Oh, absolutely. And one other thing I'd like to ask the group, guys, I know what I'm doing when it comes to writing copy, but I when it comes to being a coach in print, I'm an absolute neophyte. And so I would deeply appreciate it if you would take the trouble in the next day or two to click on the feedback button at the bottom of the total package easy, which is comments or article idea that will bring up an email form address to feedback at makethesetotalpackage.com. And let us know how we did on this call. If there were things that we didn't cover, you would have liked to just to let us know that because we'll certainly consider those things for future calls. Also, just kind of give us a feeling of how we're doing and any suggestions that you would have for us as to how we might improve. You'd be doing me a great service. I'd appreciate it. Ray, well, Clayton, I thought I thought it was very valuable. I learned a lot. In other words, from my vantage point, I'm just a six-figure copywriter. So anytime I can learn from a seven figure, I'm happy as a clam. Julie, I want to thank you for making all this happen. Thanks. No problem. Actually, I did want to say as I am monitoring the questions that have come through, people seem really pleased. Good. So let's talk about offline what we can do to continue and answer everyone's questions because I think there's a lot of unanswered questions. And Clayton, you have a list of 21 things that you want to talk about. And I have a list of 10, 15 things I want to ask you. Great. All right. All right. Thanks, guys.
